data.title: Hexcel Corporation: Operating Leverage Finally Arrives, Strong Buy
data.description:
Hexcel Corporation (HXL), the provider of composite materials for the aerospace and defense industry, reported Q1 2026 financial results that surpassed the estimates of analysts on the top and bottom line. The stock price has increased nearly 12% since my last report, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 3.5% return. While we see end market growth, there is also cost inflation risk, as I discuss in this report in support of my continued buy rating.

Hexcel Operating Leverage Kicks In

In the fourth quarter, Hexcel sales rose 9.9% and 8.8% on constant currency to $501.5 million. Operating income grew 30.3% to $57.6 million, with margins improving from 9.9% to 13.5%. This clearly signaled operating leverage, but we note that operating expenses as a share of revenue were higher year-on-year, with SG&A accounting for 9.9% of revenues compared to 9.5% a year ago and R&D accounting for 3.5% of revenues compared to 3% a year ago. In other words, we are seeing improving sales leverage on the gross margin level, but operating cost absorption is not yet happening in a favorable way.

Commercial aerospace sales during the quarter grew 18.8% to $332.7 million, driven by the two wide body programs, namely the Airbus A350 and Boeing 787, and two single aisle programs, which are the Boeing 737 MAX and Airbus A320neo. The Airbus A350 and Boeing 787 programs have high-value composite shipset values, while the Boeing 737 MAX and Airbus A320neo are value drivers through volume. For the twelve-month trailing period, we see that sales rose 4.5% and are at a multi-year high. Having the big picture in mind is good, but we also note that by presenting twelve-month trailing figures, the company may at times not highlight quarterly sales improvements enough. This was such a quarter, as LTM figures show 4.5% growth while quarterly figures show nearly 19% growth.

Hexcel has embedded its shrinking Industrial business into the Defense and Space segment. I believe that is not a wise thing to do, as these are distinctly different markets. Defense, Space and Other sales decreased 4.3% to $168.8 million, with the sales decline driven by the divestiture of an Industrial business in Austria in September 2025 and lower space composite sales for launchers and rocket motors, while defense sales were lifted by fighter jet and military helicopter programs.

2026 Is Shaping Up To Be A Good Year But With Some Risks

For the full year, Hexcel is expecting $2-$2.1 billion in sales; this would mark high single-digit to low-double-digit sales growth. EPS is expected to be $2.10-$2.30 with free cash flow bigger than $195 million, marking around 25% growth in free cash flow generation.

Generally, 2026 is shaping up to be a good year for Hexcel as gross margins are improving with the potential for better operating cost absorption. At the same time, we note that the war in Iran is bringing some risks. Higher oil prices could raise energy and logistics costs as well as feedstock costs. On propylene, which Hexcel uses for composite manufacturing, the company hedges for eight quarters, meaning that the company is partially shielded in the near term from higher input costs. The company also uses a local-for-local setup where facilities in Europe produce for the European market and facilities in the US produce for the US market. Similarly, their inputs are also concentrated in the areas of production, limiting immediate supply chain risks.

Hexcel Corporation Remains Attractive on Aerospace Growth

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I am upgrading Hexcel Corporation stock to a strong buy with a $105.52 price target, indicating 16% upside. That may not seem like upside that would justify a strong buy rating. However, we note that improving commercial airplane production rates in combination with growing defense demand brings the sales CAGR to around 10%, with EBITDA growing 17% annually through 2028 and free cash flow growing 26% annually. So, the operating leverage is expected to sharply improve while Hexcel trades at a discount to peers.

EBITDA margins dipped in 2025 due to destocking activities among commercial airplane manufacturers as production rate increases were softer than expected. However, the current expectation is that Hexcel will be able to substantially increase its operating leverage, and that would drive margins to north of 20% with strong free cash flow generation opportunities and capital expenditures moderating against operating cash flow. My assumption is that the company will maintain share repurchase activity while simultaneously also seeing its net debt leverage improve,

Conclusion: Hexcel Is Finally Showing Operating Leverage

For Hexcel, there are some cost inflation risks due to the war in Iran, but I do not expect demand to fall through. So, we are watching the cost development, but note that Hexcel has partial shielding against rising costs. Furthermore, we see that operating leverage is substantially improving as airplane production rates increase, and that should be a key driver to free cash flow. I am upgrading Hexcel from "buy" to "strong buy" on its strong growth prospects while being undervalued against peers.

data.title: Alphabet Q1 Preview: Why The $4 Trillion Market Cap Still Offers Some Value
data.description:
My last analysis of Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) (check it here) was at Q4 earnings, and since then the stock dropped a bit but has already recovered and is up about 8%. And now with Q1 approaching (next week, on the 29th), it seems like the best time to revisit Alphabet’s thesis, assessing risks and mainly thinking about whether this valuation of more than $4 trillion makes sense.

To jump to my conclusion, I do think it makes sense, mainly in the long run. Alphabet has truly proven that it deserves this “new” valuation with a certain premium instead of being a stock that trades at a massive discount compared to other big techs, as was the case a few years ago. But at the same time, I am a bit more cautious about what the market may think in the Q1 earnings, since with this volatile environment and a higher valuation level, drawdowns also become somewhat more likely.

Alphabet’s Valuation Still Makes Sense

In my article “Betting Against Alphabet? You’ll Need A Better Bear Case.” I attached the following table (check the full article here):

Even with recent advancements, I do not think it is a completely impossible scenario, but certainly in the short term this scenario has already become obsolete and unlikely. Search has shown very healthy growth, subscriptions as well, and Cloud delivered an impressive growth. Q4 was excellent, and with all the investments Alphabet is making in infrastructure, this growth should continue for some time.

So I improved my outlook for the coming years. As you can see in the table below, I brought Search assumption back to a more moderate scenario, growing 10% in 2026 and declining to mid-single digits. I kept similar expectations for Subscriptions, Network, and YouTube, and for Cloud I increased growth considerably given the scale of investments I mentioned.

But as you can see, the multiples (price-to-operating income) are higher than before. This is because now the market is assigning a relevant premium to Alphabet; therefore, the market cap increased, and multiples also increased.

Still, even in this scenario of a $4 trillion market cap, I do not think Alphabet is extremely overvalued or anything like that. The ~17x operating income in 2030 assumes a very weak Search momentum. If AI continues proving to be a tailwind without cannibalizing revenue for 5 years, we are talking about something closer to 16x-15x, and margins could also continue improving and exceed the estimated 35%. It is worth remembering that operating income here also acts as a proxy for shareholder value. In 2030, Alphabet may end up generating more cash flow than it does now since depreciation and other expenses do not impact cash flow, just as it may generate less cash for shareholders if CapEx remains high.

The market expects even better numbers than these, which reinforces how these assumptions are somewhat conservative. Revenue for 2030 is close to $770 billion, and the difference is mainly that the market assumes stronger momentum for 2028-2030, keeping numbers above 10% even in that period. With that, the market sees a P/E of 15.5x for 2030.

As I mentioned, my table is more of a proxy; it is not something I want to get exactly right but rather to assess the margin of safety. And even in this scenario that I consider between conservative and base, Alphabet still makes sense in valuation terms, since 17x operating income for a company leading in AI, growing EPS in low double digits or high single digits, with tons of optionality, is cheap.

Think that if in 2029 GOOGL stock is trading at 25x forward operating income, that is a 43% appreciation in 3 years. It is not extraordinary, but it is already interesting.

Q1 Earnings: What Could Go Wrong

But the reason I am a bit more cautious is that I think the path to 2030 will have turbulence.

One reason is a mix of higher valuation and the market’s short-term focus. Even though I am bullish on Alphabet’s case and also on Q1, I think the market may end up disliking some aspects.

The first and most obvious is CapEx. In the last Q4, Alphabet already mentioned the large amount of CapEx it intends to spend this year, and Q1 will be a quarter where we will get a taste of that. Even though the 2025 CapEx was not low, free cash flow was still quite positive. Cash from operations was $164 billion, and CapEx was $91 billion. The same happened in Q4. Now think of a scenario where, in Q1, CapEx alone consumes ~80%-100% of cash flow from operations; the market may react negatively and trigger a sell-off.

Currently, GOOGL stock is trading at a forward P/E close to 30x. Just returning to the average of 23x would imply a drawdown of around 20%. If the market really dislikes the results, we could see part of this “full drawdown potential.”

This would be negative for the stock, but personally it would have very little impact on my long-term view of Alphabet.

But this is not the only thing that could go wrong in Q1. Google Search remains very important, so if numbers come in much worse than what we have been seeing recently, or if there is any indication that AI is no longer a tailwind for the search business—that is, if they say Gemini is growing but cannibalizing search, that would be concerning in the medium term. In fact, it is even more complex than that. Even if AI revenue starts to appear, for example, subscriptions with Gemini, but margins cannot keep up due to high costs, we may have greater uncertainty in Alphabet’s case and again a potential drawdown.

Even news about regulation, such as updates on legal cases or any one-off impact on the financials could happen.

Q1 Earnings: My Take Is That It Will Be Solid

But even with this concern about perception, I think Q1 earnings will be solid. Particularly, I think what would be most interesting as a positive surprise is Search. If it maintains solid growth or even accelerates slightly, proving that “AI mode” and new Google features are increasing engagement and consequently revenue, that would be very bullish for the stock.

Another point is Google Cloud. Even if CapEx is a delicate issue, if this business division maintains strong top-line growth, with healthy margins and a strong backlog, it will be an excellent signal that the business is sustainable and that demand is real and consistent.

Just these two points could already drive a double beat for Q1.

The market expects revenue of $106.9 billion, 18% growth. In Q4 Alphabet not only delivered 18% YoY growth but also delivered $113 billion in revenue. So even considering seasonality and similar factors, delivering something close to $110 billion seems completely possible.

For EPS, the market expects some decline due to the comparison base. But even here Alphabet may surprise if there is any EPS trigger, such as an updated valuation of SpaceX as seen in previous quarters, lower losses from optionalities such as Waymo, or even better Cloud margins.

In short, I am optimistic about Q1. I believe Alphabet will be able to show that the infrastructure they are building is not in vain, that the Cloud business is indeed growing sustainably; also prove that the core is not only solid but growing; and finally, they will provide good operational updates in the earnings call, whether on Search, YouTube, Subscriptions, or even “Other Bets,” that further strengthen the long-term case.

Bottom Line: Short-Term Volatility, Long-Term Strength

Well, as I believe is clear, in the short term anything can happen. Even if Alphabet delivers a double beat next week, nothing prevents the market from pushing the stock down 5% just because it dislikes pressured free cash flow or something similar. And this becomes even more true when Alphabet’s multiples are already above historical averages, so yes, I have a cautious tone.

But while I expect volatility in the short term, I expect strength in the long term. I would not see a problem if the stock does not rise or even declines somewhat, as long as results remain solid and management can increase my conviction that the long term is promising. Therefore, I maintain my buy rating on Alphabet stock.

data.title: Whale's Insight: If BTC's Top Was Pulled Forward, Why Not The Bottom?
data.description:
The four-year cycle is mutating. Volatility is compressing, returns are shrinking, and the old playbook no longer fits. But behind the surface, institutions are building positions through ETFs, equity, and preferred stock. Meanwhile, on-chain LTH supply is rising again and shorts remain under pressure.

The Mutating Cycle: What Institutions Are Telling Us

The Four-Year Cycle Is Mutating

Measured from the April 2024 halving, Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) current cycle has dramatically underperformed every prior epoch.

The 2012 cycle delivered roughly 9,300% in gains, the 2016 cycle around 2,950%, and the 2020 cycle about 760%. By contrast, the rally from the fourth halving peaked at roughly 97% above the halving-day price, an order-of-magnitude step down from any predecessor.

This is not just a matter of smaller percentage moves on a larger base.

The 30-day realized volatility tells a parallel story:

  • 2020 cycle peak: 9.64%

  • 2024 cycle peak: 3.11%

  • Current reading: 1.75%

Drawdowns are compressing as well. Previous bear markets routinely saw 80–90% peak-to-trough declines, whereas the current cycle's deepest retracement from its all-time high above $125,000 to roughly $60,000 represents a drawdown just above 50%.

Each successive halving cycle is delivering lower volatility, shallower drawdowns, and smaller upside. These are signature traits of an asset class maturing and absorbing deeper pools of institutional capital.

What makes this cycle structurally different is that Bitcoin broke to a new ATH before the halving, not after.

In every prior cycle, the halving occurred while Bitcoin was still well below the previous peak. In this cycle, the approval of U.S. spot Bitcoin ETFs in January 2024 pulled the breakout forward, with BTC surpassing its prior high before the halving even took place.

This front-running of the supply event reshuffled the cycle's internal clock. If the top was pulled forward, why not the bottom?

  • Scenario A: Already in. The $60,000 area tested earlier this year may have already marked a significant support zone for this cycle.

  • Scenario B: Still ahead, but sooner. The trough arrives between now and Q4 2026, potentially below $60,000, driven by evolving macro conditions and shifting market narratives.

  • Scenario C: On schedule. The lowlands around Q4 2026 or later, broadly in line with historical four-year templates.

The structural shift demands that market participants prepare for multiple scenarios rather than anchoring to a single cycle narrative.

The Institutions Behind Strategy's Accumulation

Strategy (MSTR) disclosed on April 20 its third-largest single BTC purchase on record: 34,164 BTC for $2.54 billion.

  • Total holdings: 815,061 BTC

  • Cumulative cost: $61.56 billion

  • Avg. cost: $75,527

Year-to-date, Strategy has added roughly 80,000 BTC, surpassing BlackRock's IBIT to become the single largest BTC holder globally.

The engine behind this pace is STRC; it funded over 85% of the latest purchase. With plans to shift STRC distributions from monthly to semi-monthly, Strategy is tightening the feedback loop: more frequent payouts attract more capital, which flows directly into more Bitcoin. It is, in effect, a self-reinforcing accumulation machine.

The more important question is not how much BTC Strategy is buying, but who is funding the vehicle that enables it.

Wall Street's largest institutions are operating through multiple channels simultaneously.

  • Institutional investors collectively own roughly 53–60% of MSTR.

  • Strategy's Executive Chairman Michael J. Saylor personally holds 19.99M shares of Class B stock (9.9% ownership, 45% voting power), further aligning insider incentives with the accumulation strategy.

  • On the STRC side, the holder base is similarly institutional: iShares Trust (BlackRock), American Funds, Vanguard ETFs, and Fidelity ETFs are among the top holders.

This creates a multi-layered institutional exposure structure. These firms are not merely issuing their own spot Bitcoin ETFs; they are simultaneously holding Strategy equity and purchasing STRC, effectively channeling capital into Bitcoin through multiple vectors at once.

Institutions are not just holding. They are actively adding, even with Bitcoin in the $60K–$75K range. In just the past week:

  • Capital Group: +$747M in MSTR (40% increase), total position now above $1.78B

  • Vanguard (VOE): +1.21M MSTR shares ($195M), total position now $255M

Short-term price action can be misleading. But the capital structure tells a different story: the world's largest asset managers and investment banks are not reducing exposure. They are building it through increasingly sophisticated, multi-instrument channels.

Institutional and BTC LTH Are Building Positions

ETF Flows: Strong and sustained institutional inflows.

Spot BTC ETFs have decisively flipped back to net inflows since early April. As of April 21, BTC ETFs have recorded 6 consecutive days of net inflows, with April 17 posting the strongest single-day inflow of 2026 at +$663.9M.

Spot ETH ETFs (ETH-USD) show a similar trend on a smaller scale. 9 consecutive days of net inflows out of the past 12 trading days, the longest streak of 2026. Total net assets have recovered from $10B during the February drawdown to $14B.

On-chain: Long-term holders are re-accumulating.

Since early April, Bitcoin's Long-Term Holder (LTH, >155 days) total supply has begun trending upward again. In the past 7 days, LTH supply has shown a notable increase, now sitting at approximately 16M BTC.

Historically, this pattern signals that experienced holders view current prices as attractive, and it has preceded significant rallies in prior cycles. For reference, the two most recent LTH supply peaks occurred in late September 2024 and late June 2025. In both cases, BTC saw meaningful upside afterward.

Shorts Under Pressure, Second Squeeze Loading

This week's derivatives picture has been dominated by short liquidations.

The largest came on April 17, when Iran ceasefire progress and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz triggered a rapid move from $73K to $78K, resulting in the week's heaviest short squeeze.

Across the week, short liquidations have consistently outpaced longs, confirming that the prevailing leveraged positioning was against the rally.

The conditions for a second squeeze are building. Two indicators suggest the market remains structurally loaded on the short side:

  1. Funding rates remain negative. The OI-weighted funding rate has been persistently negative since mid-April, though it is gradually compressing toward zero. Looking across timeframes: 30-day avg. -0.319%, 7-day avg. -0.074%, 1-day -0.012%. Shorts are slowly bleeding, paying to maintain positions that are increasingly under water.

  2. The long/short ratio (by account) is still below 1. And the Top Trader long/short ratio at 0.55, indicating institutions remain net short.

The $79–80K zone is a strong resistance level where institutional shorts are heavily concentrated. The first attempt this week failed to break through, pulling back to $78.3K in a typical post-squeeze repricing phase.

However, BTC is currently highly correlated with U.S. equities and sensitive to macro headlines, meaning a breakout is entirely possible. Whether price can hold above $80K, however, will largely depend on how the macro picture evolves.

Week Ahead

  • Apr 28-29: FOMC meeting and press conference

  • Apr 28: Robinhood Q1 2026 earnings release after market close

  • Apr 29: Meta, Microsoft, and Alphabet earnings releases after market close

  • Apr 30: U.S. Q1 2026 GDP advance estimate and March PCE inflation release

Next week’s tone will be shaped by the interaction between macro policy signals and large cap earnings risk.

The Fed remains the primary volatility event, but the market will also need to digest a dense earnings cluster from Robinhood, Meta, Microsoft, and Alphabet across April 28 and April 29. That combination matters for crypto because it directly affects broad risk sentiment, AI and cloud leadership expectations, and the strength of retail trading activity.

The April 30 GDP and PCE releases would then become the confirmation layer, determining whether the post-event move evolves into continuation or reverses into a broader risk reset.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

data.title: EUR/USD Drifted Down To 1.1665/1635 Key Support For Potential Bullish Reversal
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The EUR/USD (EUR:USD) hit a recent two-month high of 1.1849 printed on last Friday, April 17, 2026, and turned soft due to the stalled second round of US-Iran peace talks, where it declined by 1.5% to hit a low of 1.1669 on Thursday, April 23, 2026.

Secondly, cracks have appeared in the extended ceasefire agreement, where both sides are using force to prevent oil tankers from transiting the Strait of Hormuz, which led to a 10% plus rally in oil prices, increasing the appeal of short-term safe haven demand status on the US dollar.

Interestingly, the EUR/USD’s five-day decline from its recent two-month high has reached an inflection area for a potential bullish reversal from a technical analysis and intermarket perspective.

Let’s uncover these factors in greater detail.

Eurozone/US implied interest rate policy curve spread has steepened

The monthly implied future policy interest rate curves for the eurozone and the US are calculated using short-term interest rate futures that are highly sensitive to the expectations from these countries’ central banks' monetary policies

The current eurozone/US implied interest rate policy curve spread for the period from May 2026 to September 2026 has steepened from three months ago, with the current September 2026 reading standing at -1.32% compared to -1.37% three months ago (see Fig. 1).

These observations suggest that the ECB is likely to be less dovish or more hawkish than the Fed, which in turn could provide support for a potentially firmer EUR/USD.

Let’s now focus on the potential short-term trajectory (1-3 days) of EUR/USD.

EUR/USD - Minor uptrend phase from March 13, 2026 low remains intact

Despite the five-day decline seen in the EUR/USD, its price actions are still trading above the 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving averages.

Watch the 1.1665/1635 key short-term pivotal support, and a clearance above 1.1722 (potential upside trigger) may see the next intermediate resistances coming in at 1.1790, 1.1835, and 1.1890 (also a Fibonacci extension) (see Fig. 2).

On the other hand, failure to hold at 1.1635 and an hourly close below it invalidates the bullish reversal scenario to see a continuation of the corrective decline to expose the next intermediate supports at 1.1575 and 1.1510.

Key elements to support the near-term bullish bias on EUR/USD

  • The five-day decline of the EUR/USD has reached the intersection area of the 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving averages, where the 20-day MA is now shaping an impending bullish crossover above the 50-day MA.
  • The price actions of the EUR/USD have continued to oscillate within its minor ascending channel in place since the March 30, 2026 low.
  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator has just shaped a bullish divergence condition at its oversold region on Thursday, April 23, 2026.

Original Post

data.title: Clearway Energy: My 3 Valuation Frameworks Show Too Little Yield For The Risk
data.description:
Introduction

Clearway Energy (CWEN) is a US infrastructure company with a portfolio of renewable assets and some dispatchable assets (based on gas) as well. It makes its revenues mainly from long-term power purchase agreements, so the cash flow is stable. However, because it is heavily front-loaded on capex, and therefore heavily depreciated, reported earnings can give the wrong picture.

This is why, when CWEN is (typically) framed as an income play — a utility-like business with a ~5% yield — it misses how the stock should actually be valued. Not fully on dividends, and certainly not on earnings — CWEN should be valued more as heavily leveraged, with valuation shaped by three things: enterprise multiples, forward cash generation, and yield compression.

Current valuation levels seem bullish. Management says they are looking to target ~$2.70 Cash Available for Distribution (CAFD’) per share in the medium term. CWEN trades at a premium to its own historical EV/EBITDA range. Nothing looks extreme; however, taken together, CWEN seems to be valued as if it is on a clean execution path towards growth, stability, and financing conditions.

This is why we will do a 3-pronged structured valuation exercise on CWEN — EV/EBITDA, dividend yield, and CAFD-based — to see if the current market valuation leaves any room for upside.

Clearway — my valuation approach

Clearway is neither a traditional utility nor a growth stock. Its goal is long-term, contracted cash flow, and renewable infrastructure is just the way to get there. Clearway has wind, solar, and battery storage assets, and most of its revenue comes from long-term (average - ~12 years) power purchase agreements (PPAs). This gives them current visibility at the cost of future upside — earnings don't suddenly accelerate in this business.

That is why earnings (EPS’) is not the right metric to judge Clearway. Earnings gets distorted — by depreciation, financing structure, and asset-level accounting — so we must focus on CAFD. It reflects the cash actually generated after operating costs, interest, and maintenance capex. This is where dividends are paid from.

One other defining feature is how CWEN grows. It is not organic, unlike typical operating companies. It doesn’t go and acquire customers. Growth comes from a) acquisitions of operating assets, b) hand-me-downs from its sponsor (Clearway Energy Group), and c) expanding its existing fleet.

This needs a lot of capital. Access to reasonably-rated financing is a key to sustaining growth.

The final aspect is that the balance sheet takes on a disproportionately important role here than in most cases. With so much debt, CWEN behaves like a leveraged spread vehicle. On the one hand are the interest rates it has to service. On the other hand are the contracted returns on assets purchased using that debt. The spread is the residual cash that goes to shareholders. This is why interest rates and financing conditions play such an outsized role in valuation.

Valuation Framework #1: EV / EBITDA (The Anchor)

This is the first place to start. EV/EBITDA captures the value of the underlying assets independent of its debt-equity structure. This also avoids being confused by the EPS, which is largely irrelevant here anyway.

Current valuation

In 2025, CWEN generated ~$1.217B of adjusted EBITDA. Using the current enterprise value of ~$17–18 billion, the stock is trading at about 17.0x EV/EBITDA on a trailing basis and 12.3x on a forward basis. This is actually slightly above the sector median and tells us something very important: CWEN is not being priced as a distressed or high-risk yield vehicle.

Historical context

We should compare CWEN with its own historical numbers, not just the sectors. Why? — because, first, CWEN works very differently from most utilities and even from many renewable peers, with its contracted yieldco-like structure compared to regulated utilities. Second, a historical outlook shows us how the company’s valuation has changed over time through different rate regimes. Third, it also shows how the market has valued CWEN’s time-dependent risk profile.

So, historically, CWEN has traded closer to around ~14x EV/EBITDA on a trailing basis, compared to ~17x today. This gives us a clear reference point. At today’s ~17x EV/EBITDA, CWEN is trading about ~19% above its 5-year average of ~14x, indicating a clear premium to its historical valuation.

What reversion looks like

Take EBITDA constant and apply historical multiples. At the above 5-yr average, EV would decline from ~$17.8B to ~$14.9B (17.82 ÷ 17.06 × 14.29 = ~14.9). Take out net debt of ~$9.2B, and equity value is ~$5.7B. Compared to the current market cap of ~$7.9B, this is a ~25-30% downside if the stock were to go back to its historical multiples.

Interpretation

What we see here is that CWEN is trading strongly above its historical range. There’s a ~19% premium at the enterprise level, which, because of leverage, translates to a much larger ~25–30% downside at the equity level if multiples normalize.

This premium comes because the market expects that CAFD will continue to grow, financing will remain stable, and demand for yield-oriented infrastructure assets will stay strong.

Importantly, these assumptions are not purely theoretical. Clearway has already signed ~2 GW of PPAs with hyperscalers, and PPA pricing today is roughly double what it was three years ago. The company also continues to demonstrate strong access to capital markets, having recently raised $600M of debt at attractive rates and issued equity without meaningful disruption. In addition, its ~30 GW development pipeline provides significant redundancy relative to what is required to meet its 2030 targets.

Taken together, these factors support forward CAFD growth and help explain why the market is assigning a premium valuation.

However, that is precisely the issue. With these positives already reflected in the price, there is limited room for an execution gap.

Valuation Framework #2: Dividend Yield

While EV/EBITDA tells you how the market is valuing the assets, the dividend yield tells you how the market is pricing the stock as an income vehicle.

Current yield

With a current share price of ~$39.88, and a forward annual dividend of about $1.84, yield is ~4.80%. This places CWEN firmly in the income-asset category, but at a lower end of the yield spectrum.

What the yield reflects

This below-5% yield in a structure like this is not accidental. This tells us that the market is pricing CWEN for:

  • Cash flow visibility (long-term contracted PPAs)

  • Moderate growth (CAFD expansion toward ~$2.70/share)

  • Relative stability versus higher-risk yield vehicles

So, that means the market isn’t pricing CWEN as a volatile high-yield security. It is being priced more like a “quality income compounder”—despite carrying meaningful leverage.

Yield vs. reality

But this creates a tension. The current US 10-year Treasury yield is ~4.30%, which means CWEN’s ~4.8% is not a huge compensation to shareholders for all the risk they are taking here, including equity risk, leverage, and so on. On top of that, the yield is also trying to compensate shareholders for the cost of future growth, which involves large external financing and the risks/rates associated with it.

Given its risk profile, I would say the market is being quite thrifty with the yield. From here, the stock can only go higher if the dividend somehow manages to grow faster than expected or if the yield compresses even more. That second thought means investors are willing to accept even lower returns for the same risk. Both are possible, but neither is going to be easy. That’s because CWEN is already more or less correctly valued as a safe, high-quality income asset. This doesn’t give it much room for yield compression. So unless there’s a sudden and unexpected spurt in growth, upside stays limited.

Valuation Framework #3: CAFD

We have said that EV/EBITDA gives us an assessment of the assets’ worth, and yield says how the market prices the stock based on income. In that vein, CAFD, we can say, tells us what the business is actually generating for its shareholders. It is the metric management uses to run the company. It is also what ultimately supports both valuation and dividends.

CAFD trajectory

Clearway reported $430M of CAFD in 2025, with guidance of $470–510M for 2026. More importantly, management continues to target ~$2.70 CAFD per share by 2027, with a longer-term path toward ~$2.90–3.10.

This is steady growth, at around mid-single-digit to high-single-digit. This is not explosive growth, driven as it is by slow-building items like acquisitions, project commissioning, and sponsor drop-downs.

What the current price implies

Given the current share price and the CAFD/share target, CWEN trades at ~14.5x forward CAFD. This is an important multiple. It is based on forward, not current, cash flows. Also, assumes the company will properly execute its growth pipeline. In other words, the multiple comes not from what CWEN is earning today but (correctly) from what it is expected to deliver over the next few years.

Dividend coverage and reinvestment

With a forward dividend of ~$1.84, CWEN is distributing roughly 68% of CAFD (based on forward estimates). That leaves a meaningful portion of reinvestment, but not enough for stronger growth. For that, they need to rely on external capital. Nothing wrong here — this is typical of the structure. But it again shows how the model depends on capital markets.

What we see from this CAFD framework is that the current valuation already discounts future growth, not present performance. That means, if CAFD grows as expected, valuation holds. If either it slows or interest rates rise, valuation compresses. Again, from this perspective, there is very little upside.

Putting It Together

All three frameworks show the same/similar thing. EV/EBITDA shows CWEN is trading at a premium to its historical range, and the premium comes from both quality and visibility. The dividend yield framework shows that CWEN is priced like a stable income asset. However, given the risk profile, the yield is relatively tight. Lastly, the CAFD valuation shows that forward growth is already priced in, not just current cash flow.

Taken individually, none look extreme. Taken together, they assume a tight execution path, with no room for errors. The path includes the target that CAFD will scale towards ~$2.70/share and beyond, that capital costs will stay reasonable, and that investors will continue to find income-oriented renewable assets attractive.

What to Watch in the Next Earnings

Clearway declares earnings in around 2 weeks, and there are several things to watch there. The first item on my list is CAFD guidance. Any deviation from the original numbers will impact valuation. Secondly, capital costs and financing — if these increase, future returns as well as valuation multiples will come under pressure.

Guidance of growth is also an item to watch. Management commentary on acquisitions, sponsor drop-downs, and so on is critical. The company depends on adding assets consistently. A slowdown here would weaken the forward CAFD.

Lastly, we have external demand, mainly from AI/data centers. If the company discloses new contracts tied in to these hyperscalers, that will be very strong support for the current premium. In short, reported earnings are less of a focus here. Growth and financing are the key watchlist items.

Risks to Valuation

There are several risks to valuation. CWEN may be a solid enough business, but given the premium multiples being assigned, many large and small things could force a re-rating.

The first risk is the interest rate. Valuation here is closely tied to rates. First, higher yields make the stock’s yield less attractive — the gap isn’t much to begin with anyway. Second, higher rates increase the cost of financing new assets. Thus, if rates rise, they create a double impact.

A related risk is the company’s high leverage — which is even higher than its equity value. In a stable environment, the leverage works fine. However, it amplifies refinancing risks, makes CWEN more sensitive to interest rates, and, if capital tightens, it restricts growth. In such a scenario, its required returns are higher, which weakens EV/EBITDA. It also puts pressure on how sustainable the dividends are.

CWEN doesn’t grow organically. Much of its growth is dependent on capital markets. This is how the model is, and this is a structural risk. If capital becomes costly, CAFD slows, forward assumptions break down, and all this directly impacts the CAFD-based valuation framework.

These risks, as well as policy changes impacting renewables, pressure multiples. The business may still run well; however, if any of these risks actually occur, the stock trades back towards its historical valuation range — and as I have shown, that creates a meaningful downside.

Conclusion

Clearway is doubtless a high-quality, cash-generating infrastructure business, but the stock is currently priced for steady execution rather than upside. Across the three frameworks I discussed, one theme runs common — this is a valuable business, and nobody is selling it for cheap. The stock trades at a premium to its historical range. The yield is modest relative to the risk profile — high leverage, extreme rate sensitivity.

As such, despite having nothing to criticize CWEN on purely operational grounds, I rate it a Hold.

2026 Q1: The rep account dropped 4.59% net of fees in Q1 2026, vs. a loss of -4.35% for the S&P 500, a gain of 0.92% for the Russell 2000, and a decline of -3.10% for MSCI ACWI; all total returns. Simply put, the place to be in Q1 2026 was natural resources, with the Bloomberg Commodity Index +23.3%. Not having exposure to that segment of the market, along with a stronger USD, hindered our results in Q1.

Still, if the value-based investment discipline to which I adhere is valid and my ability as a practitioner sufficient, the idiosyncratic aspects of our portfolio should far outweigh the various macro headwinds or tailwinds over time. So, while this highly global (82% of portfolio is non-U.S.) approach lately seems dominated by the ups and downs of the USD currency, with the strong USD weakening our unhedged results in Q1 2026, I do not expect our returns to be perpetually pressured in the absence of USD weakness. Yet, I remain a bit wary of most USD assets, mainly on valuation, as the positioning implies.

Artificial Intelligence (A.I.) is top of mind in markets around the world in 2026, and it may well live up to the expectations supporting the unprecedented amounts of capital being invested in developing it and the high valuations awarded to the perceived leaders, companies both public and private.

Then again, I recall after the COVID crash of March 2020, the video-conferencing software company Zoom (ZM) (NASDAQ: ZM, $79) soared from $67 on 12/31/19 to $589 on 10/23/20 on rampant expectations that we'd all be using Zoom (and seemingly nothing else) instead of business travel going forward. And while Zoom did grow tremendously, at its $589 peak the valuation was $173B, more than triple the combined market caps of the big four major U.S. airlines (DAL, UAL, AAL, LUV) at about $50B around that time. Today the market cap. of Zoom is $25B while those four airlines combined are over $100B. People continue to fly for many business meetings, despite the ease of jumping on a Zoom call.

I think a similarly wrongful extrapolation is creating opportunities today in companies misperceived as being doomed by the competition that A.I. may enable. In most cases, I think these companies will be beneficiaries, not victims of the great things that A.I. can do for us all. A.I. should more likely enhance H.I. (Human Intelligence) rather than replace it. The overdone selling for fear of disruption from A.I. was exacerbated by another source of concern which is the war with Iran which began on 2/28/26. The mild market sell-off that ensued made some of the names on my watch list cheap enough to buy.

I plan to describe the investment thesis for each of the new holdings in my next letter, but feel free to inquire directly via phone or email if you would like to know more sooner. I expect they will become larger weightings in the portfolio as this year progresses.

The biggest contributor to our 19% gain in 2025, AMA Group Ltd., with nearly 4 percentage points of contribution from a 54.1% gain, was also the biggest detractor from performance YTD as of 4/10/26, with its 34.4% YTD loss costing 3.24 percentage points of performance, without which our -2.25% YTD loss through 4/10/2026 would have been a 1% gain.

At roughly AUD 0.50 (USD 0.35) now, AMA's stock price is valuing Australia and New Zealand's largest collision repair firm by far, with over AUD 1B in sales, at a measly enterprise value of AUD $235M (excluding leases), which is only 3.1x the EBITDA (after lease expenses) of AUD $75M expected in calendar 2026. I think fair value is triple the current price, which would be an enterprise value of 9.5x EBITDA for c2026 (AUD $75M), and 8.4 EBITDA for c2027 (AUD $85M).

AMA's share price was declining well before the Iran conflict began, from an interim peak of AUD 1.10 (USD 0.72) in October 2025, despite producing steadily improving results in-line with their forecasts and analysts' expectations. The pre-war weakening of the share price might have been due to sellers taking profits from the huge issuance of new shares back in July 2024 at a split-adjusted AUD 0.42, which while dilutive did at least put the company in a nearly debt-free (excluding leases) position.

AMA dropped nearly 30% in AUD terms, -32% in USD, during the month of March as the war in Iran caused energy prices in Australia and New Zealand to surge, which the market correctly fears might lead to reduced miles driven (and thus fewer collisions to repair) if it continues for much longer. Australia is unusually vulnerable in this regard, given almost all of its fuel is imported, and they have very little in reserves. But even if gas lines form and rationing ensues, it should be months not years.

There were long gas lines near where I lived in Old Brookville, New York, in 1979 due to the Iranian revolution shutting down Iran's oil production back then, and the Iran vs. Iraq war that followed. I was 11 years old and waiting for an hour, sometimes more, in the back of my Mom's car for our turn to get gas. Cars with even-numbered license plates could get gas on even-numbered calendar days, and odd numbered only on odd dates. But like the initial gas crisis in 1973-1974, it only lasted a few months.

Insider buying at AMA Group, as with a few of our other holdings like Mattel, has been encouraging. AMA director Brian Austin most recently bought another 1.5M shares at AUD 0.614, more than 22% above the current price, on March 10 th & 12 th . He now owns just over 10M shares.

Mattel's Chairman and CEO, Ynon Kreiz, bought another 65,000 shares at $15.53 ($1.79M) on 2/12/26, given him 1,794,217 shares ($26M) in total now. Mattel Films' " Masters of the Universe " movie will be released on June 5, 2026, and hopefully He-man and Skeletor can create a merchandising boom like Mattel's " Barbie " movie did in 2023.

And shortly after we bought our new position in Italy's dominant payments firm Nexi Spa (NEXPF) (NEXI: IM) at €2.85 (USD 3.27) on 3/18 and 3/20, Nexi promoted from within a new CEO, Bernardo Mingrone, who bought 327,868 shares at €3.02 (just under €1M) on 3/26, the day he was appointed and spoke of a focus on capital returns, which is encouraging. At our cost Nexi's stock has a 10% dividend yield with a 20%+ free cash flow yield.

After briefly being surpassed by Canadian bus maker, NFI Group (NFYEF) (NFI:CA), as our largest holding, Aimia Inc. (AIM:CA) is back to our largest weighting as I sold some NFI Group in Q1 into its recent rally to fund new holdings. Aimia's stock price was flat (0% return) YTD through 4/10/26, and almost flat in 2025 save for a weakening USD which gave the CAD denominated shares a boost in USD terms.

Aimia had a bit of a setback in Q1 when it announced the sale of its largest asset, the Italian specialty chemical company Bozzetto, which Aimia agreed to sell for much less than the analysts who cover Aimia and I thought it was worth. I had estimated that Bozzetto was worth a minimum of 8x EBITDA but it was sold for what appears to be about 6.5x EBITDA, an enterprise value of C$411M with net proceeds to Aimia of C$265M. That reduces my NAV per share estimate for Aimia from C$5.00 to C$4.00, which while painful is still 47% higher than the C$2.73 closing price on this Friday, Apr. 10 th .

On the bright side, the sale of Bozzetto might not have happened at all had Aimia held out for a better price, as the war with Iran which broke out less than three weeks after the Bozzetto sale was announced has caused input costs to soar (crude oil, naptha, etc.) so the timing was fortuitous. Getting Aimia some cash to invest, potentially during a bear market cycle (the sale should close in May), might yield much more than what seems to have been left on the table in the Bozzetto sale. Sometimes it is necessary to take a step back before attempting a bigger leap forward.

After a rough start, 2025 turned out well for our investment performance. I think by again having been prepared to take advantage of opportunities as they present, usually quite inconveniently and sometimes all at once, we have set the stage for 2026 to be even better.

I look forward to sharing more details about the new holdings soon and again I welcome any inquiries in the interim.

Sincerely,

Christopher P. Mittleman, Chief Investment Officer

Mittleman Value Partners LLC

data.title: CGO: Positioned For Continued Outperformance Of Foreign Markets
data.description:
The Calamos Global Total Return Fund (CGO) is a closed-end fund that aims to provide its investors with a high level of both capital appreciation and current income. The fund does reasonably well in terms of current income, as its 7.64% current yield is higher than most alternatives that investors might consider for income along with global equity exposure. Unfortunately, though, this fund’s performance against global equity indices has been rather disappointing, as it has been underperforming in recent years. With that said, though, this fund’s performance has been good enough that investors who care more about income than total return might still be willing to consider it. Its performance has also been improving in recent months, which might be an encouraging sign. The global exposure here is also nice, as foreign markets have been outperforming American ones over the past year or two, and as such, investors with foreign exposure have enjoyed greater wealth accumulation. Let us look more closely at this fund in order to determine whether or not it is worth considering for new money today.

About The Calamos Global Total Return Fund

The website for the Calamos Global Total Return Fund states that it has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of capital appreciation and current income. The fund seeks to achieve this objective by investing in what its manager describes as a “broad range of security types:”

This is not really surprising for a closed-end fund managed by Calamos Investments. As investors who are familiar with this fund house are likely aware, Calamos funds frequently include a combination of common equities, convertible bonds, and high-yield bonds, among other financial instruments (such as bank loans, preferred stock, and occasionally structured products). We can see that there is certainly a fairly wide variety of asset types here, and this is certainly not unusual for this fund. A core aspect of Calamos Investment’s strategy is to use whatever assets the management believes will allow the fund to deliver the highest possible total return, regardless of what types of securities they are. With that in mind, we might wonder why the fund does not just invest entirely in common stocks. After all, most financial websites and financial advisors consistently state that the stock market outperforms all other financial assets.

In fact, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has outperformed common stocks since the start of the 21st century. On December 29, 2000, which was the last trading day for gold in the twentieth century, the yellow metal closed at $272.65 per ounce. The closing price on April 22, 2026, was $4,729.44 per ounce. That is a 1,634.62% return since the start of the century. On December 29, 2000, the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR) was at $1,807.13. The index closed at roughly $15,930 on April 22, 2026. That is a 781.51% total return for common stocks since the start of the 21st century, assuming that all dividends paid out by all of the constituents of the S&P 500 Index were reinvested over the period. Clearly, common stocks have not been the best-performing financial assets over the past 25 years. However, as is the case with most closed-end funds, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund does not invest in physical gold. It only invests in securities such as common stocks and bonds. It can, however, include shares of gold mining companies. The fund had 0.99% of its portfolio invested in Alamos Gold (AGI) as of February 28, 2026, for example. However, unlike a fund such as ASA Gold & Precious Metals Limited (ASA), investing in gold miners is not the primary focus of this fund.

At this point, one might once again suggest that a portfolio consisting entirely of common stocks should deliver the highest returns out of all types of securities. Historically, that has been true over extended periods of time, but there have been periods in which other types of securities have outperformed common stocks. We can actually see this simply by looking at the performance of various types of securities over the past decade. This chart shows the performance of the S&P 500 Total Return Index, the iShares Convertible Bond ETF (ICVT), and the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) over that period:

Over the ten-year period, common stocks did deliver the highest total returns. However, prior to the COVID-19 pandemic era, we can see that the total return performance of common stocks and convertible bonds was almost identical. In 2020 and 2021, convertible bonds were actually outperforming common stocks by a fairly significant margin. In addition, historically, certain types of bonds have actually outperformed common stocks during recessions and panics. As such, while it is true that common stocks tend to be the highest-performing financial securities over the long-term, there are other assets that can do better during certain economic or market environments. This is one reason why financial planners typically recommend that individuals do not simply invest entirely in common stocks as a way of saving money for retirement. A portfolio consisting of various types of financial securities can actually be advantageous to own over the long term because of the fact that one type of security can do well when another is performing poorly. In addition, we cannot always predict in advance which asset will do the best. For example, there were not very many people back in April 2023 who predicted that gold would outperform both the S&P 500 and large-capitalization technology stocks (XLK) over the next three years.

As such, it can be a good idea to run a diversified portfolio with exposure to several different asset types. This approach appears to be consistent with the strategy under which the Calamos Global Total Return Fund operates.

The largest positions in the fund’s portfolio also show the fund’s general diversification across asset types. The website provides the following list of the fund’s top ten holdings as of March 31, 2026:

We see a combination of common stocks, convertible bonds, and even a convertible preferred security issued by Boeing (BA.PR.A). This reinforces the fact that the Calamos Global Total Return Fund is not an equity fund that holds an allocation to fixed-income securities like some other closed-end funds that we have discussed in this column. Investing in convertible securities is a core component of this fund’s strategy. In fact, Calamos Investments is one of the largest convertibles management companies in the United States, and most of its funds include a hefty allocation to convertible securities. The best-performing funds from this manager, however, have a portfolio that has more than half of its assets invested in common stocks alongside a sizable convertible sleeve. This combination still provides the fund with most of the upside potential of common stocks, along with a higher income than it would earn from a portfolio that consists entirely of common equities.

There are some changes in the fund’s portfolio since we last looked at it a year ago. Several of these changes are for the better. For example, the fund’s largest positions list no longer includes the SPDR Blackstone Senior Loan ETF (SRLN) or the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN). The SPDR Blackstone Senior Loan ETF was not even in the fund’s portfolio at all on February 28, 2026, but BKLN still accounted for 0.49% of its assets at that time. These securities were both 2.3% of the fund’s net assets on March 31, 2025, so it appears that the fund has been divesting itself of these positions. I will admit that I like this move, which is not likely to be surprising to regular readers. As I have pointed out in past articles (such as this one), senior loans tend to be a very poor asset to hold during monetary easing periods. The latest guidance from the Federal Reserve predicts that the effective federal funds rate will be at 3.4% by the end of 2026 and 3.1% by the end of 2027, which would require a single 25-basis-point interest rate cut in both years:

The central bank does not predict any increases in the federal funds rate in the near future, although the market is actually priced for a very small chance of a 25-basis-point increase this year:

As of right now, the market thinks that there is a 0.4% chance that the Federal Reserve will increase interest rates this year, but the majority opinion still very much favors either no change or a cut by the end of the year. The market and the Federal Open Market Committee both strongly believe that there will be one or two 25-basis-point cuts by the end of next year. Thus, despite some concerns that sprang up last month that the rising oil prices will force the Federal Reserve to switch back to a monetary tightening regime, neither the market nor monetary policymakers believe that. The overwhelming consensus points to continued monetary easing.

Bank loans are not a good asset class to hold whenever a nation’s central bank is easing monetary policy. This is because these securities usually have a variable-rate coupon that pays out an amount of money that is determined by short-term interest rates. In the United States, the yield of these securities is generally equal to the average Secured Overnight Financing Rate plus a specific percentage. The Secured Overnight Financing Rate itself tends to very closely correlate with the federal funds rate. Thus, whenever the Federal Open Market Committee cuts interest rates, the Secured Overnight Financing Rate declines, and the coupon payments paid out by bank loans decrease. Unlike bonds, bank loans do not rise in price whenever interest rates decline. As such, the attractiveness of bank loans as holdings decreases in response to monetary easing. As the Federal Reserve is widely expected to continue to ease going forward, CGO almost certainly has better options than holding ETFs comprised of bank loans. The fund’s management made a good decision divesting these securities.

The Calamos Global Total Return Fund also reduced its exposure to Southern Company’s (SO) and Spotify USA’s (SPOT) convertible bonds, as well as Amazon’s (AMZN) and JPMorgan’s (JPM) common stock since the last time that we looked at it. In their place, we have Samsung (SSNLF), Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Hyundai Motor (HYMLF), Iberdrola Finanzas (IBDSF), ASML Holding (ASML), and UniCredit S.p.A. (UNCFF). The addition of Samsung Electronics is not particularly surprising, as Samsung has been one of the best-performing common stocks in the market over the past year or so. Samsung Electronics is a major producer of computer memory, which has benefited from a surge in demand as various technology companies construct data centers and computer hardware to support their efforts in developing artificial intelligence. This surge in demand has actually caused a shortage of certain types of memory and driven up the prices of this vital computing component. Samsung Electronics’ common stock is up 290.48% on the South Korean KRX exchange over the past year.

This has greatly outpaced most other stocks in either American or global markets over the same period. As a result, any fund that owned it prior to the start of this year (it is up 81.40% year-to-date) has seen its representation as a proportion of assets increase. As such, many global closed-end funds have Samsung among their largest positions in the portfolio right now.

The incredibly strong performance of Samsung over the past several months may understandably raise questions about how sustainable the current price is, and how well the stock will be able to hold onto its gains. After all, the rapid appreciation was caused by a shortage of electronic components, and shortages rarely last forever. Back in January, the Consumer Electronics Show was held in Las Vegas, Nevada, and this event gave memory purchasers the opportunity to discuss the shortage with suppliers (PC World published an article mid-January discussing this event). Based on these discussions, it is very possible that the current memory shortage will continue through at least the end of 2027. The markets are forward-looking, so it is quite possible that Samsung’s stock will begin to decline once investors have confidence that the current tightness of memory supply will begin abating. However, that still gives the Calamos Global Total Return Fund plenty of time to realize the gains that it has earned in this stock. There is no guarantee that it will be successful in timing its exit, but it still might start taking some gains off of the table before the memory shortage is resolved.

The Calamos Global Total Return Fund Versus Index ETFs and Peers

The Calamos Global Total Return Fund has a 7.64% yield at the current share price. This yield compares favorably to the yields of any of the major broad-market American or global equity indices. We can see that in this chart:

As we can see in the chart above, both of these indices have far higher yields than the ones that include American stocks. This strongly suggests that foreign stocks have, on average, higher yields than American ones. Further evidence of this can be found by looking at the yields of the country-specific indices of those countries that are included in the portfolio of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund. As clearly shown, all of the non-American country-specific index ETFs have higher yields than the one tracking American equities, except for the one tracking Japanese stocks. This supports our previous assertion that non-American stocks have higher yields than those issued by companies based in the United States. This suggests that income-seeking investors may wish to have a higher level of exposure to non-American stocks than most of the global indices do, as the higher yields that are available outside of the United States will allow an investor to earn more current income given a specific amount of principal.

The Calamos Global Total Return Fund could be following this suggestion. As the chart above shows, the fund has 47.7% of its assets invested in the United States. This would, naturally, give it a 52.3% allocation to foreign assets. This is higher than either the MSCI World Index or the MSCI All-Countries World Index possesses:

As shown, both of these indices, which are supposedly global, have less than 40% of their value in non-American stocks. That is far out of proportion to the representation of the United States in the global economy (the United States only accounts for about 27% of global gross domestic product), which could suggest that the American equities in general are overpriced relative to the rest of the world. The Calamos Global Total Return Fund is a bit more balanced in this respect, although it also has an outsized exposure to the United States relative to its actual representation in the global economy. The more important thing here, though, is the higher yields that non-American stocks have relative to American ones. As this fund has a higher level of exposure to non-American assets than the indices, the effective yield that it earns from its equity portfolio should be higher than the indices earn. The fact that this fund also holds convertible bonds and junk bonds, which also both have higher yields than common stocks, should result in its portfolio having a higher effective yield than any of the global equity indices. This should prove to be appealing to just about any income seeker.

The fund’s American exposure has come down a lot since the last time that we looked at it. At the time that my previous article was published, this fund had 57.30% of its assets invested in the United States. This suggests that this fund has been benefiting from the outperformance that foreign markets have been delivering over the past year or so. This chart shows the performance of American equities (VTI) against the ACWX Index since the start of 2025:

As we can see, the international index fund is up about 40.64% since the start of 2025. This was a far better performance than the 21.19% gain that American equity indices delivered over the same period. The fact that the Calamos Global Total Return Fund has a higher level of exposure to foreign markets than the indices do should have been a benefit for it in this situation because it was better positioned to capture foreign market gains. While this fund did have greater American exposure the last time that we discussed it, the 57.30% weighting towards the United States that CGO had in April 2025 was still lower than the indices.

The fund’s higher level of international exposure relative to the global indices also should have benefited the fund so far this year. This chart shows the price performance of the Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF against the ACWX index year-to-date:

As we can see, the outperformance of foreign markets relative to American ones is still ongoing. It was not a one-time event. As CGO is more heavily weighted to foreign stocks than domestic ones, this should position it well against the indices.

The fund’s positioning has indeed allowed it to deliver superior performance. Since the start of 2026, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund has delivered an 11.41% total return, beating both VTI and ACWX:

However, this fund did not do quite as well in 2025. This fund has only managed to deliver a 22.46% total return since the start of 2025. That was pretty similar to the performance of VTI over the same period, but the fund greatly underperformed the MSCI All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index:

It is possible that the junk bonds in the portfolio of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund held it back last year. The fact that the fund had greater exposure to American equities in the early stages of 2025 might have also been a drag on its performance. There are some other funds on the market that have outsized exposure to non-American common stocks and similar bond exposure that substantially outperformed VTI since the start of 2025. For example, the Thornburg Income Builder Opportunities Trust (TBLD) has delivered a 49.03% total return since the start of 2025:

Regardless of the cause of the fund’s underperformance versus ACWX, though, it does appear that the Calamos Global Total Return Fund has altered its positioning to take advantage of continued outperformance of foreign stocks. The fact that the fund has beaten both indices year-to-date stands as evidence of this.

Of course, we should note that this same change could hurt the fund should American stocks once again start to outperform those issued by entities located outside of the United States. I will admit that I do not see a catalyst for such a change to the current trend, but most estimates of gross domestic product growth do have the United States delivering greater growth than the majority of developed market nations over the next year.

While the 7.64% yield of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund is quite attractive compared to most index funds, it is not nearly as attractive when compared to closed-end funds that typically have much higher yields. We can see that in this peer comparison chart:

This may be disappointing for investors whose primary goal is to generate a high level of current income. After all, the higher the yield of a given fund, the higher the amount of income that it will provide. There are several peer funds here that provide their investors with higher amounts of income than CGO.

With that said, Calamos closed-end funds tend to be lower-yielding than other closed-end funds on the market, but they deliver stronger capital appreciation in exchange. It is not unusual to see a fund from this fund house have a low yield compared to similar funds but deliver higher total returns. The Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund (CSQ) is a good example of such a fund, as it only has a 7.60% yield, but it has actually beaten the S&P 500 Total Return Index over the past decade. The above numbers assume that an investor in each of the funds used the distributions to purchase more shares on the distribution payment date, so we can clearly see that CGO’s lower yield relative to its peers did not really handicap the fund’s ability to provide its investors with a respectable return.

Recent Performance

The last time that we discussed the Calamos Global Total Return Fund was on April 30, 2025. As such, almost a year has passed since the last time that we looked at this fund. We have already discussed some of the important developments that have occurred since that time that actually affect this fund, so let us just have a look at the fund’s performance since that time.

Shares of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund are up 20.31% since our discussion, underperforming the MSCI All-Countries World Index:

However, if we take the distributions paid out by this fund over the period, we see that it very slightly outperformed the index:

The fund’s outperformance was not consistent, though. As shown, there were several time periods during which this fund was underperforming the index. That may not be surprising given its different composition. As already mentioned, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund has exposure to both convertible bonds and junk bonds that have different return profiles than global common stocks. The fund has sufficient exposure to these securities to alter its portfolio’s performance. In addition to this, CGO uses leverage, which can potentially enhance returns, but it also increases the fund’s volatility. We can see that in the chart above, as CGO tends to experience larger declines than does the index whenever common stock prices decline.

Distribution Analysis

The website for the Calamos Global Total Return Fund states that the fund has the primary objectives of providing its investors with both capital appreciation and current income. In accordance with the current income objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.08 per share ($0.96 per share annually). This gives the fund a 7.64% yield at the current share price. This fund has historically had a reasonably stable distribution, as the last time that it changed was back in October 2022, when it cut the payout by $0.02 per share monthly. It has kept it stable at $0.08 per share per month since that time. This may be nice for those investors who are looking for a stable source of income, but we should still have a look at the fund’s finances to see just how sustainable it truly is.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report for the Calamos Global Total Return Fund is the annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025. This is a newer report than the one that was available to us the last time that we looked at this fund, so let us review this document and see how well CGO did at covering the payout.

For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund reported a net investment loss of $1,270,969. This was obviously not enough to cover any distribution, but the fund still paid out $9,434,202 to its investors over the period.

Fortunately, this fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund reported net realized gains of $10,766,298 along with net unrealized gains of $12,862,989. The fund’s investment portfolio produced investment profits of $22,358,318 over the period:

This was more than the fund paid out in distributions over the period. In addition, this fund managed to cover its distributions solely through net realized gains despite the net investment loss. For the twelve-month period, the total of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund’s net investment loss and net realized gains was $9,495,329. That was more than the $9,434,202 that the fund paid out. Thus, it managed to cover its distributions just with its realized profits, albeit barely. Still, though, this is the ideal situation, and as such, it does not appear that this fund is distributing more than it can actually sustainably afford.

Valuation

Shares of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund are currently trading at a 0.96% discount on net asset value. This is a substantially less attractive price than the 10.30% discount on net asset value that this fund’s shares have had on average over the past year.

The current price of this fund is also somewhat less attractive than its long-term average. Over the past five years, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund has traded at prices ranging from a 12.00% discount to a 23.81% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period has been a 2.97% discount on net asset value. As the five-year average is a larger discount than this fund has today, we can assume that it might be possible to wait a little bit for a more attractive price to emerge.

Here is how the current price of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund compares with its peers:

One thing that we immediately notice is that most of these funds are trading at prices that are actually higher than the intrinsic value of their assets. The Calamos Global Total Return Fund is one of the few exceptions to this, as it is at a slight discount. There is only one fund out of the peer group that is currently trading at a cheaper price (the Thornburg Income Builder Opportunities Trust). While this does point to the fund being reasonably priced relative to its peers, that does not necessarily mean that it is a good buy right now. As we have already seen, CGO has historically traded at larger discounts than the fund has today. In this light, it might be best to wait until the discount widens before purchasing shares.

Expenses

The fund’s most recent annual report states that the Calamos Global Total Return Fund had an expense ratio of 4.02% in the most recent period. However, as is the case with most closed-end funds, the bulk of this fund’s expenses comes from the fact that it uses leverage and has to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed. If we exclude these expenses, the fund’s expense ratio was 1.78% for the most recent period.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund is a closed-end fund that provides investors with a way to get exposure to non-American common stocks along with a higher level of income than most index funds can provide. The fund is not the highest-yielding one around, though, as there are other global hybrid funds with higher yields. This fund appears to be reasonably well-positioned to profit should foreign assets continue to outperform American ones, as the fund is underweight to the United States relative to the indices. This has likely been a contributing factor in this fund’s exceptionally strong performance year-to-date. However, the current price is less attractive than this fund’s typical price, and as such it might make sense to wait for a better entry point if you want to purchase the fund.
